codeSnapshot.label: l1_regularization.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# L1 regularisation in PyTorch (manual — no built-in for L1 in optimizers)
model = nn.Linear(10, 1)
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01)

lambda_l1 = 1e-3

for epoch in range(100):
    x = torch.randn(32, 10)
    y = torch.randn(32, 1)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss = nn.MSELoss()(model(x), y)
    # L1 penalty: add |w| to loss
    l1_penalty = lambda_l1 * sum(p.abs().sum() for p in model.parameters())
    total_loss = loss + l1_penalty
    total_loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

# Elastic Net: L1 + L2
lambda_l1, lambda_l2 = 1e-3, 1e-4
for epoch in range(100):
    x, y = torch.randn(32, 10), torch.randn(32, 1)
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss = nn.MSELoss()(model(x), y)
    l1 = lambda_l1 * sum(p.abs().sum() for p in model.parameters())
    l2 = lambda_l2 * sum((p**2).sum() for p in model.parameters())
    (loss + l1 + l2).backward()
    optimizer.step()

# Check sparsity after L1 training
with torch.no_grad():
    weights = model.weight.data
    sparsity = (weights.abs() < 1e-4).float().mean()
    print(f"Fraction of near-zero weights: {sparsity.item():.2f}")

eli3: L1 regularisation (also called Lasso in linear regression) adds the sum of the absolute values of all weights to the loss function. This forces the model to not only fit the data but also keep weights small.

The key property of L1: it drives some weights exactly to zero. This produces sparse models — most weights are zero, meaning most features are ignored. The model automatically performs feature selection, keeping only the most useful features.

Why zero exactly? The absolute value function has a sharp corner at zero (not smooth like L2). This corner creates a strong force pulling weights all the way to zero rather than just making them small.

whenWhy.title: Automatic feature selection in high-dimensional data
whenWhy.body: When you have many features (p >> n), L1 regularisation identifies the most important subset. In genomics, you might have 20,000 genes as features but only a few hundred relevant ones. Lasso automatically zeroes out irrelevant gene coefficients, producing an interpretable model with a small active set. The selected features have direct scientific meaning.

whenWhy.title: Compressed sensing and sparse signal recovery
whenWhy.body: L1 minimisation is the foundation of compressed sensing: recover a sparse signal x from few measurements y = Ax (fewer equations than unknowns). If x is truly sparse (few nonzero components), L1 minimisation exactly recovers x when A satisfies the Restricted Isometry Property. This is used in MRI acceleration (fewer measurements → faster scan), signal denoising, and neural network compression.

whenWhy.title: Interpretable models with feature coefficients
whenWhy.body: In clinical risk scores or credit scoring, the model must be interpretable: each risk factor is either present (nonzero weight) or absent (zero weight). Lasso naturally produces such models. The resulting coefficient vector directly identifies which variables matter — unlike L2 regularisation which only shrinks coefficients without zeroing them.

deepDiveIntro: The L1-regularized loss adds a penalty proportional to the sum of absolute weight values to the data loss. For linear regression this is Lasso. Unlike Ridge, Lasso has no closed-form solution, but coordinate descent solves it efficiently by updating one weight at a time using soft-thresholding: weights smaller than the threshold get set to exactly zero. [1]

Geometrically, the L1 constraint restricts the weight vector to lie inside a diamond shape in parameter space. Because the diamond has corners aligned with the coordinate axes, the optimal solution is likely to land at a corner where most weights are exactly zero. This geometry explains why L1 produces sparsity where L2 does not. [1]

deepDive.heading: Gradient and Soft-Thresholding
deepDive.body: The L1 penalty pushes each weight toward zero with a constant force equal to the regularization strength, regardless of how small the weight already is. The proximal update — called soft-thresholding — subtracts this amount from the weight's magnitude and clips the result at zero: weights whose magnitude falls below the threshold are set to exactly zero, while larger weights are simply shrunk by the threshold amount. [1]
deepDive.formula: Soft thresholding: wⱼ ← sign(vⱼ) · max(|vⱼ| − ηλ, 0)

deepDive.heading: L1 vs L2 Regularisation: Geometry
deepDive.body: L2 penalizes the sum of squared weights, so its gradient shrinks as weights shrink — small weights receive a smaller push and never reach exactly zero. L1 penalizes the sum of absolute weights, so its subgradient has constant magnitude regardless of weight size — small weights still get the full push toward zero. Geometrically, the L2 constraint region is a smooth sphere while the L1 region is a diamond with corners on the axes; the optimal solution is more likely to land at a corner under L1, producing sparsity. [1][2]
deepDive.formula: L_Lasso = L_data + λΣ|wⱼ|;  L_Ridge = L_data + λΣwⱼ²

deepDive.heading: Coordinate Descent for Lasso
deepDive.body: Lasso is solved by coordinate descent: cycle through each weight, hold all others fixed, and update the current weight to minimize the objective analytically using soft-thresholding on the partial residual. Repeat until convergence. This is the algorithm behind scikit-learn's Lasso. Path algorithms such as LARS can trace the full sequence of solutions from completely regularized (all weights zero) to unregularized, which is useful for model selection. [2]

deepDive.heading: Elastic Net: L1 + L2
deepDive.body: Elastic Net combines an L1 penalty for sparsity with an L2 penalty for stability among correlated features. Lasso tends to arbitrarily pick one feature from a correlated group; Elastic Net tends to include or exclude them together. This makes Elastic Net more stable when predictors are highly correlated. In scikit-learn, the alpha parameter controls overall strength and l1_ratio controls the mix between L1 and L2. [2]

takeaways: L_total = L_data + λΣ|wⱼ| — penalty on absolute weight magnitude. [1]
Subgradient = λ·sign(w) (constant magnitude) → drives small weights exactly to zero. [1]
Soft-thresholding: wⱼ ← sign(vⱼ)·max(|vⱼ|−ηλ, 0) — exact zeroing of small weights. [1]
L1 ball is a diamond: optimal solution hits a corner where most weights are zero. [1]
Elastic Net = L1 + L2: sparse like Lasso but handles correlated features better. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why does L1 produce exactly zero weights while L2 doesn't?
edgeCases.a: For L2: the gradient of λwⱼ² is 2λwⱼ. As wⱼ → 0, the gradient → 0. So there's no force pushing the weight through zero — it just gets very small but never exactly zero. For L1: the subgradient of λ|wⱼ| is λ·sign(wⱼ) for wⱼ ≠ 0 — constant force toward zero regardless of magnitude. When the weight is small enough (|wⱼ| < ηλ), the subgradient step overshoots zero, but the soft-thresholding proximal operator clips it to exactly zero. This exact zeroing is a fundamental property of L1 that L2 cannot replicate.

edgeCases.q: How do you tune λ in Lasso — cross-validation or information criteria?
edgeCases.a: Standard approach: k-fold cross-validation over a grid of λ values (scikit-learn: LassoCV). LassoCV uses a path algorithm (LARS) to efficiently compute solutions for all λ values simultaneously, much cheaper than fitting separately. AIC/BIC are alternatives: AIC = n·log(MSE) + 2·df where df = number of nonzero coefficients. AIC tends to select more features than CV; BIC is more conservative. In practice, CV is preferred because it directly optimises held-out prediction error. The 'one standard error' rule: choose the most regularised model within 1 SE of the minimum CV error — adds conservatism and simplicity.

sources.label: Tibshirani, R. (1996) — Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso, JRSS-B 58(1):267–288
Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 3
